origin: A fragment of Koine Greek biblical genealogy, listing paternal successions in the lineage leading to Jesus; it matches the Matthew 1 genealogy (Zerubbabel → Abiud → Eliakim → Azor) as preserved in Greek scriptural traditions (e.g., Septuagint/NT Greek manuscript transmission).
author: Not an individual “author” in the modern sense. Most likely an ancient biblical translator/copyist (or the Greek text-tradition of Matthew) transmitting the genealogy from the source text; if one must pick an attributed author, it would align with the author of the Gospel of Matthew or the scribal tradition that rendered it in Greek.
thoughts: The author/scribe is likely focused on establishing a legitimate, continuous ancestral line. The motivation is religious/historical: to demonstrate fulfillment of prophecy and connect Jesus’ identity to Israel’s past and to the Davidic lineage through documented generations.
audience: Greek-speaking Jewish/early Christian readers (or communities in the Greek-speaking Mediterranean) who accept scripture as authoritative and are interested in genealogical proof of messianic legitimacy and continuity with Israel’s history.
written_when: The underlying composition of Matthew is generally dated to the late 1st century CE (often ~80–90 CE). This specific Greek wording would have been copied/transmitted thereafter, but the fragment most plausibly belongs to that late-1st-century NT context (with manuscript copies later, into the 2nd–4th centuries CE).
